the-story-of-dhul-qarnain.script: The Story of Dhul-Qarnain. Here is the Quranic account itself, taken from the Holy Quran, chapter eighteen, verses eighty-five to one hundred. Take a moment as we read it in full. “And they ask thee about Dhul-Qarnain. Say, ‘I will certainly recite to you something of his story. We established him in the earth and gave him the means to accomplish everything. Then he followed a certain way. Until, when he reached the setting of the sun, he found it setting in a pool of murky water, and near it he found a people. We said, ‘O Dhul-Qarnain, either punish them, or treat them with kindness.’ He said, ‘As for him who does wrong, we shall certainly punish him; then shall he be brought back to his Lord, Who will punish him with a dreadful punishment, But as for him who believes and acts righteously, he will have a good reward, and We shall speak to him easy words of Our command’. Then indeed he followed another way. Until, when he reached the rising of the sun, he found it rising on a people for whom We had made no shelter against it. Thus indeed it was. Verily, We encompassed with Our knowledge all that was with him. Then he followed another way. Until, when he reached the place between the two mountains, he found beneath them a people who would scarcely understand a word. They said, ‘O Dhul-Qarnain, verily, Gog and Magog are creating disorder in the earth; shall we then pay thee tribute on condition that thou set up a barrier between us and them?’ He replied, ‘The power with which my Lord has endowed me about this is better, but you may help me with physical strength; I will set up between you and them a rampart. Bring me blocks of iron.’ They did so till, when he had levelled up the space between the two mountain sides, he said, ‘Now blow with your bellows.’ They blew with bellows till, when he had made it red as fire, he said, ‘Bring me molten copper that I may pour it thereon.’ So they — Gog and Magog — were not able to scale it, nor were they able to dig through it. Thereupon he said, ‘This is a mercy from my Lord. But when the promise of my Lord shall come to pass, He will break it into pieces. And the promise of my Lord is certainly true.’ And on that day We shall leave some of them to surge against others, and the trumpet will be blown. Then shall We gather them all together.” This single passage, as the exhibition will show, holds the key both to the story of Cyrus and to prophecies still waiting to unfold.

verses.text: And they ask thee about Dhul-Qarnain. Say, ‘I will certainly recite to you something of his story. We established him in the earth and gave him the means to accomplish everything. Then he followed a certain way. Until, when he reached the setting of the sun, he found it setting in a pool of murky water, and near it he found a people. We said, ‘O Dhul-Qarnain, either punish them, or treat them with kindness.’ He said, ‘As for him who does wrong, we shall certainly punish him; then shall he be brought back to his Lord, Who will punish him with a dreadful punishment, But as for him who believes and acts righteously, he will have a good reward, and We shall speak to him easy words of Our command’. Then indeed he followed another way. Until, when he reached the rising of the sun, he found it rising on a people for whom We had made no shelter against it. Thus indeed it was. Verily, We encompassed with Our knowledge all that was with him. Then he followed another way. Until, when he reached the place between the two mountains, he found beneath them a people who would scarcely understand a word. They said, ‘O Dhul-Qarnain, verily, Gog and Magog are creating disorder in the earth; shall we then pay thee tribute on condition that thou set up a barrier between us and them?’ He replied, ‘The power with which my Lord has endowed me about this is better, but you may help me with physical strength; I will set up between you and them a rampart. Bring me blocks of iron.’ They did so till, when he had levelled up the space between the two mountain sides, he said, ‘Now blow with your bellows.’ They blew with bellows till, when he had made it red as fire, he said, ‘Bring me molten copper that I may pour it thereon.’ So they (Gog and Magog) were not able to scale it, nor were they able to dig through it. Thereupon he said, ‘This is a mercy from my Lord. But when the promise of my Lord shall come to pass, He will break it into pieces. And the promise of my Lord is certainly true.’ And on that day We shall leave some of them to surge against others, and the trumpet will be blown. Then shall We gather them all together.

persian-king-israelite-savior.script: Persian King, Israelite Saviour. This board follows Dhul-Qarnain’s westward journey, and shows how a Persian king became the saviour of the people of Israel. The Holy Quran, chapter eighteen, verse eighty-seven, tells us: “Until, when he reached the setting of the sun, he found it setting in a pool of murky water, and near it he found a people. We said, ‘O Dhu’l Qarnain, either punish them, or treat them with kindness.’” The sun, of course, sets in the west. And it was a king of Western Turkey who attacked Cyrus in five hundred and forty-six BCE. Cyrus marched from his base in Iran and defeated an army twice the size of his own. This region, near today’s Izmir, is famous for its hot mineral springs and muddy pools; even now the ‘mud baths of Turkey’ are a centre of spa tourism, testifying to the Quran’s use of the word ‘ayn hami’ah, which means, in Lane’s lexicon, “a well or a spring or pool foul with black, fetid mud.” It is a vivid vindication of the Quran’s description. Cyrus then turned to Babylon, where the Jews lay in exile, forced to worship idols and robbed of their homeland, Jerusalem. In five hundred and thirty-nine BCE he defeated Babylon’s army at Opis, then took the city in an extraordinary fashion, diverting the River Euphrates away from the city’s water defences so that his men could wade in by night, and the city was conquered without battle. There Cyrus fulfilled the Quranic instruction perfectly, punishing the guilty and showing kindness to others. He punished the Babylonian elite — the Prince Regent Belshazzar was killed on the night of conquest, ending the Chaldean dynasty. He reversed the forced-labour policies the Babylonians had imposed on conquered peoples. He restored the religious temples of all, and decreed the rebuilding of the Temple of Solomon. And he permitted the Israelites to return to Judea and rebuild their communities. Consider the timeline: in five hundred and forty-six BCE Cyrus took Western Turkey; in five hundred and thirty-nine BCE he defeated the Babylonians; and between five hundred and thirty-eight and five hundred and thirty-six BCE, the rebuilding of the Second Temple began. Without Cyrus, the religion of Moses that later came to be known as Judaism would likely not exist today as we know it.

verses.text: Until, when he reached the setting of the sun, he found it setting in a pool of murky water, and near it he found a people. We said, ‘O Dhu’l Qarnain, either punish them, or treat them with kindness.’

the-prophecy-of-isaiah.script: The Prophecy of Isaiah. This board sets before you a prophecy spoken long before Cyrus was born, from the Book of Isaiah, chapter forty-five, verses one to six. It names him directly. Listen: “Thus says the Lord to His anointed one — mashiach — to Cyrus, whose right hand I have held, to subdue nations before him and loose the armor of kings, to open before him the double doors, so that the gates will not be shut. ‘I will go before you and make the crooked places straight; I will break in pieces the gates of bronze and cut the bars of iron. I will give you the treasures of darkness and hidden riches of secret places, that you may know that I, the Lord, Who call you by your name, am the God of Israel. For Jacob My servant’s sake, and Israel My elect, I have even called you by your name; I have named you, though you have not known Me. I am the Lord, and there is no other; there is no God besides Me. I will gird you, though you have not known Me, that they may know from the rising of the sun to its setting that there is none besides Me. I am the Lord, and there is no other…’” Note what this scripture declares. The Bible calls Cyrus its anointed one, its mashiach, its Messiah. And God calls him by his very name, and holds his right hand, though Cyrus had not yet come to know Him.

verses.text: Thus says the LORD to His anointed one (māšīaḥ), To Cyrus, whose right hand I have held To subdue nations before him And loose the armor of kings, To open before him the double doors, So that the gates will not be shut ** ‘I will go before you And make the crooked places straight; I will break in pieces the gates of bronze And cut the bars of iron. ** I will give you the treasures of darkness And hidden riches of secret places, That you may know that I, the LORD, Who call you by your name, Am the God of Israel. ** For Jacob My servant’s sake, And Israel My elect, I have even called you by your name; I have named you, though you have not known Me. ** I am the LORD, and there is no other; There is no God besides Me. I will gird you, though you have not known Me, ** That they may know from the rising of the sun to its setting That there is none besides Me. I am the LORD, and there is no other…

false-claims-of-cyrus-death.script: False Claims of Cyrus’ Death. This board takes up Dhul-Qarnain’s eastward journey, and answers the old claim that Cyrus perished on that eastern front. The Holy Quran, chapter eighteen, verses ninety-one to ninety-two, says: “Until, when he reached the rising of the sun, he found it rising on a people for whom We had made no shelter against it. Thus indeed it was. Verily, We encompassed with Our knowledge all that was with him.” After securing the western border of his empire, Cyrus advanced to the eastern border. There he fought the Massagetae Scythians, a harsh nomadic people who lived in what is today Uzbekistan and Turkmenistan, east of the Caspian Sea, across today’s Kyzylkum–Karakum desert belt. As nomads they were exposed to the unrelenting sun of Central Asia — just as the Quran describes, a people with no shelter against the rising sun. And not only this: Herodotus records that they worshipped the sun too, sacrificing animals to it. The timeline places these events around five hundred and forty-five to five hundred and forty BCE, when Cyrus established Pasargadae as his capital in Fars and then turned his attention eastward; and around five hundred and forty-five to five hundred and thirty-nine BCE, when he conducted sustained campaigns in Central Asia to secure the empire’s northeastern frontier. Now notice the Quran’s enigmatic statement after mentioning this Eastern expedition: that Allah knows fully all things. This hint indicates that some point of contention arises from the eastern journey. And so it is: some accounts claim he died after these battles. The Quran, however, situates Cyrus’ Eastern journey as only the second of three campaigns, indicating that he lived to fight on. The Greek historians give at least three differing accounts of his death. Herodotus, having explicitly chosen the story that best fits his theme of moral hubris, recounts that Cyrus was beheaded in the Eastern expedition by the tribal queen Tomyris, in fulfilment of an oath she had sworn to the sun-god. Ctesias claims Cyrus fought against a group of the Massagetae, was injured, and died later after returning home, in Persia. But Xenophon reports that Cyrus was victorious against the Massagetae and lived out a long, full life, dying years later. The Quran aligns with Xenophon’s account, and continues to narrate his story after his supposed death. And Cyrus’ well-known tomb, the stepped stone monument pictured here, stands at Pasargadae in south-western Iran — over two thousand miles away from the Massagetae — bearing silent witness that he did not die on the eastern front.

verses.text: Until, when he reached the rising of the sun, he found it rising on a people for whom We had made no shelter against it. Thus indeed it was. Verily, We encompassed with Our knowledge all that was with him.

cyrus-confronts-gog-and-magog.script: Cyrus Confronts Gog and Magog. Here we follow Dhul-Qarnain’s northward journey. The Holy Quran, chapter eighteen, verses ninety-three to ninety-five, tells us: “Then he followed another way. Until, when he reached the place between the two barriers, he found beneath them a people who would scarcely understand a word. They said, ‘O Dhu’l Qarnain, verily, Gog and Magog are creating disorder in the earth; shall we then pay thee tribute on condition that thou set up a barrier between us and them?’” From the East, Dhul-Qarnain travels to a place between two barriers — in Arabic, as-saddayn — beneath which he found a people who could not understand a word of his language. They implored him for help against the tribes of Gog and Magog, who were creating disorder in their land. To identify this people who beseeched Cyrus, we are given three clues. First, they live beneath the shadow of two mountains, or two great barriers. Second, they did not naturally speak his language. And third, they were being attacked by Gog and Magog. When we look at Cyrus’ Achaemenid Empire, at its borders and towards the north, we see the Caucasus, with great towering mountains ranging from the Black Sea to the Caspian Sea. The people below these mountains were the ancestors of today’s Georgians. Their language belonged to the Kartvelian language family, known as a ‘language isolate’, meaning that it is unrelated to any other language family — Cyrus would not have understood them. And who were their attackers? Was there really evidence that the Georgians were historically attacked by tribes who could be called Gog and Magog? Indeed there was. The Scythians were a fierce, battle-hardened, horse-riding archer people who penetrated down from modern Dagestan and Chechnya into Georgia and northern Iran. They descended principally from the Yamnaya people of the Russian steppe. The Yamnaya were an admixture of two major groups: Eastern European hunter-gatherers, and Caucasus hunter-gatherers. From around three thousand BCE the Yamnaya spread both east and west, mixing with local groups over many centuries. In the West, many of their descendants settled into Europe, becoming the Celts; others eventually became the Slavic and Russian peoples.

verses.text: Then he followed another way. Until, when he reached the place between the two barriers, he found beneath them a people who would scarcely understand a word. They said, ‘O Dhu’l Qarnain, verily, Gog and Magog are creating disorder in the earth; shall we then pay thee tribute on condition that thou set up a barrier between us and them?’

the-wall-of-cyrus.script: The Wall of Cyrus the Great. This board tells how Dhul-Qarnain built his mighty barrier — and how history bears it out. First, on the matter of tribute. The Holy Quran, chapter eighteen, verses ninety-five to ninety-six, records the exchange: “‘O Dhu’l Qarnain, verily, Gog and Magog are creating disorder in the earth; shall we then pay thee tribute on condition that thou set up a barrier between us and them?’ He replied, ‘The power with which my Lord has endowed me about this is better, but you may help me with physical strength; I will set up between you and them a rampart.’” Dhul-Qarnain refuses their money and asks only for their labour. Remarkably, it is recorded of the Colchians, who lived in present-day western Georgia, in the words of Herodotus, Book three, ninety-seven: “The following moreover had no tribute fixed for them to pay, but brought gifts, namely…the Colchians… continued to bring the gifts which they had fixed for themselves every four years even down to my own time, that is to say, a hundred boys and a hundred maidens.” No tax, just manpower — the Quran stands vindicated. Now to the building of the wall itself. The Quran, chapter eighteen, verse ninety-seven, continues: “‘Bring me blocks of iron.’ They did so till, when he had levelled up the space between the two mountain sides, he said, ‘Now blow with your bellows.’ They blew till, when he had made it red as fire, he said, ‘Bring me molten qitr that I may pour it thereon.’” The word qitr, though generally understood to mean ‘molten copper’, can also mean molten metal generally. And remarkably, at Pasargadae, Cyrus’ royal centre, there is strong evidence that this was precisely how the builders joined blocks without mortar: by cutting grooves within the stones, placing iron clamps in the grooves, and pouring molten lead over them to secure the joints against rust. As for where the wall was needed, there were two routes of attack open to the Scythians. One was the Darial Gorge, through the Caucasus mountains. The other went around the mountains, between the end of the Caucasus range and the Caspian Sea. Both were guarded by great fortifications built by the Persians to block northern invaders. By the sea stood the Wall of Derbent; and the Darial Gorge, too, had a great wall and fortification system built by the Persians. Either, or both, may have originally been built by Cyrus himself to keep out the Scythians.

verses.text: ‘O Dhu’l Qarnain, verily, Gog and Magog are creating disorder in the earth; shall we then pay thee tribute on condition that thou set up a barrier between us and them?’ He replied, ‘The power with which my Lord has endowed me about this is better, but you may help me with physical strength; I will set up between you and them a rampart.

The following moreover had no tribute fixed for them to pay, but brought gifts, namely…the Colchians... continued to bring the gifts which they had fixed for themselves every four years even down to my own time, that is to say, a hundred boys and a hundred maidens.

‘Bring me blocks of iron.’ They did so till, when he had levelled up the space between the two mountain sides, he said, ‘Now blow with your bellows.’ They blew till, when he had made it red as fire, he said, ‘Bring me molten qitr that I may pour it thereon.’

the-reality-of-faith.script: The Reality of Faith. This board closes our section on Cyrus with a single verse, from the Holy Quran, chapter forty-nine, verse fourteen: “O mankind, We have created you from male and female; and We have divided you into tribes and sub-tribes for the sake of easy recognition. Verily, the most honoured among you in the sight of Allah is he who is the most righteous among you. Surely, Allah is All-Knowing, All-Aware.” Here is the reality of faith. Our division into nations and tribes, the Quran teaches, was never a hierarchy — it is only for the sake of easy recognition. True nobility lies not in birth or lineage, but in righteousness — the very quality that marked Cyrus the Great, a Persian king raised up to become the saviour of another people.

verses.text: O mankind, We have created you from male and female; and We have divided you into tribes and sub-tribes for the sake of easy recognition. Verily, the most honoured among you in the sight of Allah is he who is the most righteous among you. Surely, Allah is All-Knowing, All-Aware.
